critic_raw:
The design is substantially more rigorous than typical cases in this set — chronological split, merchant-level holdout, explicit warm-up period, and a genuinely strong baseline. The central unresolved flaw is the confound between sequence order and information content. The sequence model receives raw events over 90 days; the GBT receives aggregated summaries over 'monthly and short-horizon behavioral summaries.' These are not the same feature set even if derived from the same window. Any lift could be attributable to the sequence model's access to event-level granularity (e.g., exact timing of a chargeback relative to a support contact) rather than its ability to model temporal ordering per se. The design acknowledges this at the end but proposes no ablation to isolate it.

The label definition uses 30 consecutive days of post-snapshot inactivity as churn for merchants who had recent pre-snapshot activity. This is appropriate as a practical definition, but at the final 15% of the chronological test set, some churn labels will be assigned based on activity patterns that extend beyond the available data window — if the dataset ends at month 36 of a 24-month training history, merchants in the test set who churned near the end of the data may have incomplete 30-day observation windows. This right-censoring problem is not addressed.

The PR-AUC choice is sound given class imbalance, but no sample size analysis is provided. With rare churn events (typical B2B churn is 1-5% monthly), the test set needs sufficient positives for stable PR-AUC estimation. At 15% temporal holdout of 24 months of weekly snapshots, the number of churn events in test depends on churn rate and merchant count — the design does not verify this is sufficient for the precision-recall curve to be statistically reliable.
all_issues_raised: The comparison between the sequence model (raw event-level features) and the GBT (aggregated summaries) confounds model architecture with information granularity — any observed lift could be due to access to event-level timing details rather than sequence modeling capability, and no ablation isolates this.

The 30-day post-snapshot churn label definition creates right-censoring for merchants near the end of the test window — if the observation period ends before 30 days have elapsed for late-test-set merchants, their churn labels are missing or incorrect, biasing test set metrics.

No sample size analysis is provided for the PR-AUC estimate — with typical B2B churn rates of 1-5% and a 15% temporal test split, the number of positive test examples may be insufficient for stable PR-AUC estimation, producing high-variance results that cannot support a definitive verdict.
all_issues_adjudicated: The comparison between the sequence model (raw event-level features) and the GBT (aggregated summaries) confounds model architecture with information granularity — any observed lift could be due to access to event-level timing details rather than sequence modeling capability, and no ablation isolates this.

The 30-day post-snapshot churn label definition creates right-censoring for merchants near the end of the test window — if the observation period ends before 30 days have elapsed for late-test-set merchants, their churn labels are missing or incorrect, biasing test set metrics.

No sample size analysis is provided for the PR-AUC estimate — with typical B2B churn rates of 1-5% and a 15% temporal test split, the number of positive test examples may be insufficient for stable PR-AUC estimation, producing high-variance results that cannot support a definitive verdict.
